eli3: A decision tree classifier works like a game of 20 Questions. Starting from the top, it asks a yes/no question about a feature — 'Is the petal length > 2.5 cm?' — and sends you down the left branch for 'no' or the right branch for 'yes.' It keeps asking questions until it reaches a leaf node that says 'It's an iris virginica.'

Building the tree means figuring out which questions to ask and in what order. The algorithm picks the question that splits the data into the purest groups — groups where almost all examples belong to the same class.

Trees are powerful because they can capture complex non-linear patterns, handle mixed feature types, and are easy to visualise and explain. The main weakness is that they can memorise training data (overfit) if grown too deep.

whenWhy.title: Interpretability and explainability
whenWhy.body: A decision tree can be visualised as a flow chart and explained to non-technical stakeholders. The path from root to leaf is a human-readable set of if-then rules. This makes decision trees valuable in healthcare, finance, and legal settings where decisions must be justified.

whenWhy.title: Mixed data types
whenWhy.body: Decision trees handle continuous and categorical features natively without scaling, one-hot encoding, or imputation of missing values. They split on whatever feature and threshold reduce impurity most.

whenWhy.title: Feature importance screening
whenWhy.body: The split structure reveals which features drive predictions. Even when a single tree overfits, the feature importances computed from a deep tree are often informative starting points for feature selection.

whenWhy.title: Building block for ensembles
whenWhy.body: Standalone trees are rarely used in production. Their true power comes from being base learners in Random Forest (bagging of deep trees) and Gradient Boosting (sequential boosting of shallow trees). Understanding trees is prerequisite to understanding these ensembles.

deepDiveIntro: Decision tree classifiers work by recursively splitting the data: at each step, the algorithm searches every feature and every possible threshold to find the split that creates the purest child groups. The most common purity measures are Gini impurity and entropy (information gain), which give very similar results in practice.

Trees are grown greedily — each split is the locally best choice, not a globally optimal one. Full-depth trees memorise the training data, so they are then pruned back using a complexity penalty to improve generalisation on new data.

deepDive.heading: Splitting Criteria: Gini Impurity
deepDive.body: Gini impurity measures how mixed a node is. A perfectly pure node — where all examples belong to the same class — has a Gini of zero. A node with equal numbers of every class has the maximum Gini. The CART algorithm picks the split that produces the biggest weighted reduction in Gini across the two child nodes, effectively making each group as uniform as possible. [1][2]

deepDive.heading: Splitting Criteria: Entropy & Information Gain
deepDive.body: Entropy measures how mixed or uncertain the class labels are at a node — zero for a pure node, maximum when all classes are equally represented. Information gain is the reduction in entropy achieved by a split. Entropy-based splitting (used in ID3 and C4.5) tends to produce very similar trees to Gini impurity; Gini is slightly faster to compute since it avoids logarithms. [1]

deepDive.heading: Tree Growing: CART Algorithm
deepDive.body: CART grows a full binary tree by greedily finding the best (feature, threshold) pair at each node — the one that creates the purest split. Sorting each feature's values once makes this efficient: the total cost per node is proportional to the number of features times the number of examples times log of the number of examples. The tree keeps splitting until a stopping rule is hit, such as reaching a maximum depth, having too few examples in a node, or achieving insufficient impurity reduction. [1]

deepDive.heading: Cost-Complexity Pruning
deepDive.body: A full tree overfits by creating a separate leaf for nearly every training example. Pruning folds internal nodes back into leaves, trading a small increase in training error for a large reduction in variance. The cost-complexity criterion adds a penalty for each leaf in the tree; increasing this penalty forces more aggressive pruning. Cross-validation finds the penalty strength that minimises held-out error, and the resulting simpler tree generalises far better. [1]

deepDive.heading: Feature Importance
deepDive.body: Scikit-learn's default feature importance (Mean Decrease in Impurity) sums up how much each feature reduced impurity across all nodes where it was the split variable, normalised to sum to one. This is fast but has a known bias toward features with many unique values, which have more split opportunities. Permutation importance — measuring the drop in accuracy when a feature's values are randomly shuffled — is a more reliable alternative. [1]

takeaways: CART uses recursive binary splits, greedily minimising Gini impurity (or entropy) at each node. [1]
Deep trees overfit; apply cost-complexity pruning (tune ccp_alpha via cross-validation) to improve generalisation. [1]
Feature scaling and one-hot encoding are not required — trees operate on raw feature thresholds. [1]
Leaf predictions are the majority class in that leaf; predicted probabilities are the class proportions. [1]
Single trees are unstable (high variance); use Random Forest or Gradient Boosting for production models. [2]
MDI feature importances are biased towards high-cardinality features; prefer permutation importance or SHAP values. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why do decision trees overfit so easily?
edgeCases.a: A fully grown tree creates a unique leaf for almost every training example, perfectly memorising the training set (zero training error). The decision boundary is highly irregular. Fixes: limit max_depth, set min_samples_leaf, or apply cost-complexity pruning. Ensemble methods (Random Forest) address this by averaging many trees. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does a decision tree handle missing values?
edgeCases.a: CART in scikit-learn does not natively handle missing values (impute first). Some implementations (e.g. XGBoost, CatBoost) learn a default direction for missing values at each split, sending them left or right based on whichever direction reduces impurity more during training. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between Gini impurity and entropy in practice?
edgeCases.a: They produce similar trees in most cases. Entropy tends to penalise splits that result in one very impure child slightly more than Gini does. Gini is computationally cheaper (no logarithm). In practice the choice rarely matters — hyperparameters like max_depth have much larger effects. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why are decision trees called unstable learners?
edgeCases.a: Small changes in training data (a few new points, slight noise) can cause completely different splits at early nodes, changing the entire tree structure. This high variance is why bagging (Random Forest) is so effective: averaging many slightly different trees reduces variance while keeping bias low. [1][2]

edgeCases.q: Can decision trees handle multi-output classification?
edgeCases.a: Yes. A single tree can predict multiple output classes simultaneously by optimising the sum of impurity reductions across all outputs at each node. Scikit-learn's DecisionTreeClassifier accepts a 2D y array for multi-output problems. Each leaf stores the class proportions for all output labels. [1]

sources.label: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman — The Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 9
James et al. — An Introduction to Statistical Learning, Ch. 8
